content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
September 19,
1995
Joseph D. Patello
Port Attorney
Port of San Diego
P.O.
Box 488
San Diego,
CA
92112—0488
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our Yile No.
A-95—246
Dear Mr. Patello:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Port Commissioner Susan Lew under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.’! The facts set out in
this letter were provided in your July 27,
1995, request for
advice,
as well as in our subsequent August 15,
1995,
telephone
conversation.
OUESTION
May Commissioner Lew participate in governmental decisions
regarding the proposed expansion of the San Diego Convention
Center which is on property located within 300 feet of property
owned by a source of income?
CONCLUSION
V
Commissioner Lew may not participate in these decisions if
her business,
S.
Lew
& Associates,
or any other source of income
to her will be materially affected by the expansion of the
convention center.
V
V
-
-
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18995.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A—95-246
Page
2
FACTS
Commissioner Susan Lew is the majority owner of S.
Lew
&
Associates, Inc.,
a privately—owned real estate brokerage
corporation.
S.
Lew
& Associates has entered into a contract with
HSD Realty, Inc., to market and sell residential condominium units
in a building located at One Harbor Drive in San Diego.
The
contract provides that S.
Lew
& Associates will receive a
commission for each real estate transaction.
HSD Realty, Inc., is
wholly owned by HSD Venture,
which owns the building and units.
HSD Venture is
99% owned by Leucadia National Corporation,
whose
stock is publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange.
You informed me in a telephone conversation that while S.
Lew
& Associates has yet to sell any of the condominium units,
and has
not received any commission income,
S.
Lew
& Associates has been
reimbursed by HSD Realty for marketing-related travel expenses.
The condominium building is located within 300 feet of land
owned and under the jurisdiction of the San Diego Port District
(“Port District”).
The San Diego Convention Center, which is
owned by the Port District, is located on Port District land in
this area.
The Port District and the City of San Diego are
contemplating an expansion of the convention center and are in the
process of negotiating the terms of a joint powers agreement to
create a separate agency which will finance and construct the
expansion adjacent to and easterly of the present convention
center.
The expansion will not be within 300 feet of the
condominium building.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating iris
or using their official position to influence a
governmental-decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
A public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or -on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
2
! or on:
a
2/
An official’s “immediate family” are his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Advice File No. A-95-246
Page
3
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
3
!
***
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regarding to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provisions to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103(a)
and (c).)
In your letter you stated that Suan Lew is the majority
owner of S.
Lew
& Associates.
Thus, the business is both an
investment and a source of income to her.
This economic interest,
standing alone,
would create a conflict of interest.
Moreover,
the term “income” means,
among other things,
a payment received,
including but not limited to reimbursement for expenses, per diem,
or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any
person other than an employer.
Income of an individual also
includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially,
a 10—percent interest or greater.
(Section
82030.)
For public officials who receive commission income for
services rendered as a real estate broker, the Commission
attributes the source of that income to:
(A)
The person the broker represents- in the
transaction;
(B)
If the broker receives a commission from a
transaction conducted by an agent working under the
broker’s auspices, the person represented by the agent;
(C)
Any brokerage business entity through which
the broker conducts business;
and
.
The term “investment” means any financial interest in or
security issued by a business entity,
including but not limited to
common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt
instruments and any partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or
his or her immediate family.
(Section 82034.)


Advice File No. A-95-246
Page
4
(D)
Any person who receives a finder’s fee or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
(Regulation 18704.3.)
You indicated in our telephone conversation that Lew
&
Associates has yet to receive or be “promised” any commission
income for the sale of any condominium.
4
!
Accordingly, at this
time there is no source of commission income arising as a result
of a sale of a condominium unit owned by HSD Venture.
5
!
However,
Lew
& Associates has received reimbursement from HSD
Realty, Inc., for marketing—related travel expenses.
HSD Realty,
Inc.
is thus a source of income to Susan Lew.
You stated in your letter that HSD Realty, Inc., is a wholly-
owned subsidiary of HSD Venture, which is
99 percent owned by
Leucadia National Corp.
The Commission has held that an official
has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on a business entity which is a parent, subsidiary of, or
otherwise related to the business entity which is the source of
income.
(Regulation 18706.)
Accordingly,
Susan Lew may not make, participate in making,
or use her official position to influence a governmental decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable
6
! that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally,
on her business,
Susan Lew
& Associates, Inc.,
or on HSD Realty,
Inc.,
HSD Venture,
or Leucadia National
Corporation.
4/
Commission income is “promised” when a real estate transaction
is pending or in escrow.
(Lambert Advice Letter,
No.
A—93-365.)
5!
This letter only addresses the facts preseritedto us under the
contract with HSD Realty, Inc.
Susan Lew should review previous
sales transactions to determine if any other source of commission
income will be affected by the proposed convention center
expansion.
6!
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
A-95-246
Page
5
Material Financial Effect
The Commission has adopted several regulations which define
when the effect of a governmental decision is “material” for
purposes of the Act’s conflict—of—interest provisions.
Regulation
18702 sets forth the general guidelines for determining whether an
official’s financial interest in a decision is “material.”
If the
official’s financial interest is directly involved in the
decision, Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality.
A source of income is directly involved in a proceeding, if
the person or business entity initiates the proceeding in which
the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal,
or similar request or is a named party in,
or is the subject of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the
official’s agency.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
Under the facts presented, neithe HSD Realty, Inc., nor S.
Lew
& Associates are directly involved in the decision conce.rning
the proposed convention center expansiqn.
However, the
materiality test does not end there.
Susan Lew may still be
required to disqualify herself if her economic interests are
indirectly materially affected by the decision.
Regulation 18702.2 provides the standards for determining
materiality for business entities which are indirectly affected by
the decision.
The regulation provides standards which differ
depending on the financial size of the business entity.
For
example, if S.
Lew
& Associates,
was a small business,. Regulation
18702.2(g)
would require disqualification if any of the following
apply:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or
-
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.
(ReguThtion 18702.2(g).)
Applying the above to Susan Lew’s business,
Lew
& Associates,
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed convention
expansion could somehow affect the business’s gross revenues,
expenses or assets by the thresholds described above, then her
interest in Lew
& Associates,
standing alone without considering
the contract with HSD Realty, Inc., would require she disqualify
herself from participating in decisions.


Advice File No.
A—95—246
Page
6
For a larger business,
sUch as Leucadia National Corporation,
which is traded on the New York Stock Exchange, the standards in
Regulation 18702.2(a)
would require disqualification if any of the
following apply:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase or
V
decrease to the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$250,000 or more; or
V
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of
$100,000 or more;
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$250,000 or more.
-
(Regulation 18702.2 (a) •)
Thus, if the proposed convention center will affect any of
the respective businesses at the monetary thresholds set out in
Regulation 18702.2
(enclosed),
then Susan Lew must disqualify
herself.
For example, the condominium building is an asset of HSD
Ventures, Inc.,
and Leucadia National Corp.
If the value of the
building will increase or decrease by $250,000 or more,
she would
be required to disqualify herself from participating in the
decisions
the proposed convention center expansion.
Based on the foregoing, it appears that Commissioner Susan
Lew has economic interests which could be affected by the
expansion of the convention center.
If any of those economic
interests will be materially affected by the expansion of the
convention center,
as described above,
she may not participate in
those decisions.
V
V
I trust this give you some guidance.
If you have additional
questions,
or wish to discuss this, please feel free to contact
the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
V
JET/jt

regarding the proposed expansion of the San Diego Convention
Center which is on property located within 300 feet of property
owned by a source of income?

official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
